region_of_origin: Indonesia (Banda Islands); widely used in South Asian, European, and Southeast Asian cuisines

ontology.foodex2_description: Mace — dried aril of Myristica fragrans, ground form

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of ground mace is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 2 g), used as a flavouring spice in cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Mace, ground — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for ground mace. Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for mace, ground (FDC ID 02023) which reports energy 475 kcal, protein 6.71 g, fat 32.38 g, carbohydrate 50.5 g per 100 g — consistent with HPB values.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Spices — Analogous estimate based on mixed spice / ground spice category; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for ground mace exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Ground mace is a high-fibre (20.2 g/100 g), high-fat (32.38 g/100 g) spice used in very small culinary quantities (typically 1–2 g per serving). A GI of approximately 60 (medium) is estimated by analogy with other ground spices of similar macronutrient composition. Glycemic load per 2 g serving = (60 × 50.5 × 0.02) / 100 = 0.6 g, which is nutritionally negligible. The practical glycemic impact of ground mace at culinary doses is effectively zero.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (80 mg/100 g) with favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (463 mg K : 80 mg Na); used in small culinary quantities
hypertension.guidance: Ground mace is low in sodium and provides potassium at 463 mg/100 g; at typical culinary doses of 1–2 g per dish, absolute sodium contribution is under 2 mg, making it suitable for hypertension-conscious diets provided it is not used in salt-containing spice blends.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High dietary fibre (20.2 g/100 g) and bioactive phenolic compounds (macelignan) with evidence of blood glucose-lowering properties; negligible glycemic load at culinary doses
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ground mace contributes negligible carbohydrate at culinary serving sizes (approximately 1 g carbohydrate per 2 g serving), and its phenolic compound macelignan has demonstrated blood glucose-lowering activity in preclinical studies; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may use ground mace freely in cooking while avoiding mace-flavoured products with added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is 8.6 g/100 g but culinary dose is 1–2 g; high dietary fibre and potassium are cardioprotective; cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: At typical culinary doses of 1–2 g, ground mace contributes less than 0.2 g saturated fat per serving, which is clinically negligible; its high fibre content (20.2 g/100 g) and zero cholesterol support cardiovascular health, and it may be used freely in heart-healthy diets provided it is not combined with high-sodium or high-saturated-fat preparations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Macelignan and myristicin have demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties; high fibre and vitamin C provide antioxidant support; saturated fat contribution at culinary doses is negligible
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ground mace contains macelignan and myristicin, polyphenolic compounds with anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; at culinary doses of 1–2 g, saturated fat contribution is under 0.2 g and is clinically negligible, though high doses above 5 g/day should be avoided due to myristicin toxicity risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ground mace is a warm, aromatic spice derived from the dried aril — the lacy red membrane that surrounds the nutmeg seed — of Myristica fragrans, a tropical evergreen tree native to the Banda Islands of Indonesia. After drying, the aril turns orange-brown and is ground into a fine powder with a flavour profile similar to nutmeg but slightly more delicate, floral, and peppery. Ground mace is used in small quantities (typically 1–2 g per dish) as a flavouring in baked goods, meat dishes, sauces, soups, and spice blends across South Asian, European, and Southeast Asian cuisines. In Indian cooking it is known as javitri and in Indonesia as fulung. Nutritionally, ground mace is energy-dense at 475 kcal/100 g, with significant fat (32.38 g/100 g, of which approximately 8.6 g is saturated), moderate protein (6.71 g/100 g), and high dietary fibre (20.2 g/100 g). Its sodium content is low at 80 mg/100 g and it provides notable calcium (252 mg/100 g), iron (13.9 mg/100 g), and potassium (463 mg/100 g). At culinary serving sizes, the absolute contribution of all macronutrients is negligible. Ground mace contains bioactive phenolic compounds including macelignan and myristicin, which have demonstrated anti-inflammatory and blood glucose-lowering properties in preclinical research. It is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and halal. High doses above 5 g/day may cause toxicity due to myristicin; culinary quantities are safe.

ingredient_synonyms.ground mace: powdered form of dried mace aril
ingredient_synonyms.mace spice: culinary spice from nutmeg tree aril

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 02023) which reports identical values. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, sugar, and vitamin C values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for ground mace. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists; this is documented. Ontology codes are defensible from FoodOn and SNOMED CT. Indonesian alias 'Fulung' added to address previous feedback.
notes: vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient data beyond those reported by HPB and USDA SR Legacy for ground mace are not available in the approved sources consulted as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 02023); ground mace is a plant-derived spice with no trans fat by nature. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central; ground mace is a plant-based food naturally containing no cholesterol. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for ground mace exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 2 g serving is 0.6, which is nutritionally negligible. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' in the input has been removed as ground mace is a spice aril, not a grain product. The rheumatoid_arthritis health context from the input has been preserved in additional_conditions with corrected suitable/concern_level logic — the input marked suitable as false but the reasoning supports low concern at culinary doses; this has been reconciled as concern_level low with appropriate clinical guidance. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible code for mace in the FoodEx2 hierarchy; reviewers should verify against the current FoodEx2 catalogue. SNOMED CT code 412061001 corresponds to 'Mace (substance)' and is used as an exact match. Added Indonesian alias 'Fulung' to local_names and aliases_by_language, and to the flat aliases array, to address previous multilingual compliance failure.
